insights: Goal completion rate is uniformly 100% across all categorical dimensions (category, priority, location, assignment_group), eliminating variation in completion as a performance differentiator.

No categorical factor shows statistically significant influence on resolution time among the 236 valid-time records (all ANOVA p > 0.45, η² < 0.02), indicating structural parity in processing speed across all operational segments.

Resolution time distribution is highly uniform across all factors—not only in central tendency but also in volume (exactly 20–25% per group) and state balance (Closed/Resolved split varies by ≤10 percentage points), confirming absence of concentration or imbalance in service delivery patterns.

The dataset contains a severe data quality issue: 52.8% of records have negative resolution times (median −132 hours), and the timestamp swap hypothesis is weakly supported—only 45.5% of reversed timestamps yield plausible values—rendering resolution time an unreliable metric without upstream data remediation.

State ('Closed' vs 'Resolved') does not meaningfully explain the negative-time anomaly (54.4% vs 51.2% negative rates), nor does it correlate with meaningful differences in observed resolution behavior—undermining its utility as a proxy for completion quality or process stage.

The 236 valid-time records are statistically representative of the full dataset across all categorical dimensions (max distribution difference <5%, all chi-square p > 0.43), confirming that the null finding reflects true operational consistency—not sampling bias or subset distortion.
summary: The department demonstrates complete uniformity in goal completion—100% of tickets are closed, with no variation across category, priority, location, or assignment group—making 'completion rate' an irrelevant performance lever. Resolution time analysis on the valid subset (47.2% of records) confirms no factor meaningfully influences processing speed, with negligible effect sizes and non-significant p-values across all dimensions. This structural parity extends to volume distribution, state balance, and group-level variance—indicating consistent operational execution rather than hidden bottlenecks or disparities. Critically, over half the dataset contains impossible resolution times (median −132 hours), and remediation efforts cannot rely on timestamp reversal, as it only yields plausible values for less than half the affected records. Before pursuing further performance diagnostics, priority must shift to root-cause investigation of the timestamp generation and logging pipeline.
